comments:
This paper addresses a practical and understudied problem: controlling output length in neural encoder-decoder summarization models. The authors propose four approaches: two decoding-time constraints (fixLen, fixRng) and two learning-based conditioning methods (LenEmb, LenInit). The methods are clearly explained, implemented on a standard encoder-decoder with attention, and evaluated on large training data (Annotated Gigaword) and the DUC2004 evaluation set. Results show that the learning-based approaches better match desired (longer) lengths while maintaining competitive ROUGE scores, and statistical testing is used.

Strengths: the problem is well-motivated (real-world need for length control), the proposed solutions are simple, practical and incremental, and experiments are sufficient to demonstrate that learning-based conditioning can effectively control length without degrading automatic scores. The paper includes implementation details and evaluates multiple length settings.

Weaknesses / Suggestions: evaluation is limited to one training corpus and the DUC2004 task; a broader set of datasets or genres would strengthen claims about generality. The evaluation relies on ROUGE and automatic length histograms—human evaluation of fluency, grammaticality, and informativeness under different length constraints is missing. Some design choices (e.g., LenEmb vocabulary of length types, beam size differences) could use further justification or ablation. It would be helpful to analyze failure cases (how content selection changes when constrained) and the sensitivity of methods to very short/very long target lengths. Comparison to a wider range of recent summarization variants (or copy-aware mechanisms) would improve the contextual positioning of the contribution.

Overall, this is a solid, well-presented empirical study tackling a useful capability for sequence-to-sequence models. With additional qualitative evaluation and broader experimental validation the work would be even stronger.
